title: APPENDIX — Key concepts, vocabulary, and study questions

lines.text: APPENDIX — Key concepts, vocabulary, and study questions
Core concepts to lock in:
Adenovirus is a non-enveloped, double-stranded DNA virus with an icosahedral capsid about 90 nm across and a genome of about 36 kb.
The capsid is built from hexon (240 copies), penton base (12 copies), and fiber (12 copies), with several minor proteins.
The viral genome is organized into early regions (E1, E2, E3, E4) and late regions (L1–L5).
E1 is the master switch; deleting E1 cripples the virus and is the basis of first-generation vectors.
Three generations of adenoviral vectors: First generation: E1 and E3 deleted.
8 kb cargo.
Leaky residual viral gene expression, T-cell-mediated clearance of transduced cells, short-term expression.
Second generation: additional deletions of E2 or E4.
10–14 kb cargo.
Less leaky, longer expression, more complex production.
Third generation (helper-dependent, gutless, high-capacity): all viral genes deleted, only ITRs and packaging signal retained.
Up to 36 kb cargo.
Requires helper virus during production.
Producer cell lines complement the deleted viral functions.
HEK293 stably expresses E1, allowing E1-deleted vectors to replicate during production but not in patient cells.
Entry receptor for most types (including Ad5) is CAR — coxsackie-adenovirus receptor.
Penton-base RGD engages integrins for internalization.
Systemic Ad5 also uses hexon-factor X bridging to enter hepatocytes.

The immune response to adenovirus operates at five layers: pre-existing neutralizing antibodies, complement activation, innate DNA sensors (cGAS-STING, TLR9), cytokine release from antigen-presenting cells, and adaptive T cell responses against vector-derived antigens.
Pre-existing immunity to Ad5 is a major barrier in adult human populations (40–70% seropositive in US/Europe, often >90% in sub-Saharan Africa).
Strategies to address: rare serotypes (Ad26), chimpanzee adenoviruses (ChAdOx1, ChAd3), capsid PEGylation, heterologous prime-boost.
Jesse Gelsinger died on September 17, 1999, four days after receiving a first-generation adenoviral vector intravascularly for OTC deficiency.
Cause of death: massive innate immune response — cytokine storm, complement activation, multi-organ failure.

Key lessons: dose-response nonlinearity, route matters, leaky expression is a safety issue, pre-existing immunity amplifies acute reactions, conflicts of interest and protocol deviations are unacceptable.
Adenoviral COVID-19 vaccines included Sputnik V (Ad26 prime, Ad5 boost), Johnson and Johnson Janssen (Ad26), AstraZeneca-Oxford / Covishield (ChAdOx1), and CanSino (Ad5).
Collectively deployed at scales of hundreds of millions of doses.
Demonstrated room-temperature stable formulations and strong cellular immunity.
Associated rare adverse event: vaccine-induced immune thrombotic thrombocytopenia (VITT).

Adenovirus vs AAV trade-offs: Adenovirus carries much more cargo (up to 36 kb vs ~4.7 kb), produces stronger and faster immune response, has shorter duration of expression in patient cells, and is cheaper to manufacture at scale.
AAV is quieter, longer-expressing, and more suitable for chronic in vivo gene therapy.
Oncolytic adenoviruses are engineered to replicate selectively in cancer cells.
ONYX-015 (deletion of E1B-55K, intended to require p53-deficient host cells) was the prototype; its Chinese cousin H101 was approved in 2005.
The field continues to evolve toward "armed" oncolytics that deliver immune-stimulating payloads alongside selective lysis.
Adenoviral CRISPR delivery leverages the large cargo capacity to deliver Cas9, guide RNAs, and donor templates in a single vector — capability that AAV cannot match for full edit-and-replace systems.
Manufacturing has moved from CsCl gradient centrifugation (research scale) to anion exchange chromatography (GMP commercial scale).
Suspension cell culture in bioreactors has replaced adherent culture as the dominant upstream process.
Vocabulary checklist:
ITR — inverted terminal repeat.
Sequences at each end of the linear adenoviral genome; required for replication and packaging.
Retained in all vector generations.
Psi — packaging signal.
Short sequence near one end of the genome that the assembly machinery uses to recognize the genome for packaging.
Retained in all vector generations.
E1, E1A, E1B — early region 1 and its sub-regions.
The master switch for viral transcription.
Deleted in all standard vector generations to cripple the virus.
E2, E2b — early region 2; encodes viral DNA polymerase.
Sometimes deleted in second-generation vectors.
E3 — early region 3; encodes immune-evasion proteins.
Dispensable in tissue culture; usually deleted in vectors to free cargo space.
E4 — early region 4; encodes multiple regulatory proteins.
Sometimes deleted in second-generation vectors.
CAR — coxsackie and adenovirus receptor.
Primary entry receptor for most human adenoviral types.
CsCl — cesium chloride.
Used in density gradient centrifugation for classical adenoviral purification.
AEX — anion exchange chromatography.
Modern method for commercial-scale adenoviral purification.
HEK293 — human embryonic kidney 293 cell line.
Stably expresses adenoviral E1; standard producer line for E1-deleted vectors.
PER.
C6 — alternative E1-complementing producer cell line, designed to minimize RCA formation.
RCA — replication-competent adenovirus.
Contaminating revertant virus that has re-acquired E1 by homologous recombination with producer cell sequences.
Must be screened out of clinical lots.
ChAdOx1 — chimpanzee adenovirus Oxford 1.
Backbone for the AstraZeneca-Oxford COVID-19 vaccine and several other vaccine programs.
Ad26 — human adenovirus type 26.
Rare serotype with relatively low seroprevalence; backbone for Johnson and Johnson COVID vaccine and several Janssen products.
VITT — vaccine-induced immune thrombotic thrombocytopenia.
Rare but serious adverse event associated with adenoviral COVID vaccines, involving antibodies against PF4.
OTC — ornithine transcarbamylase.
Urea-cycle liver enzyme; deficient in the disease Jesse Gelsinger had.
Study questions:
Why is a first-generation adenoviral vector unable to provide long-term gene expression even though its genome persists in transduced cells?
Explain why ChAdOx1 was a more practical choice than Ad5 for a global COVID-19 vaccine campaign, and identify a limitation that ChAdOx1 still shares with Ad5.
Sketch the production scheme for a helper-dependent (gutless) adenoviral vector.
What component does the helper virus supply, and how do you keep the helper virus out of the final product?
Given a hypothetical 8 kb gene of therapeutic interest, would you choose AAV or adenovirus for in vivo delivery?
Justify with at least three considerations.
The Jesse Gelsinger trial dosed by direct intravascular infusion into the hepatic artery.
Explain how each of dose, route, and pre-existing immunity contributed to the catastrophic outcome.
An oncolytic adenovirus has its E1A gene placed under control of a tumor-specific promoter (the telomerase promoter, hTERT).
What does this design achieve, and what failure modes might it have in practice?
Explain why adenovirus's traditional "disadvantage" of transient expression turns out to be an advantage for CRISPR delivery.
Describe the differences in immune response between an enveloped virus and a non-enveloped virus like adenovirus, and connect this to why adenovirus is a "louder" vector.
A pharmaceutical company plans to develop a second adenoviral vaccine using the same serotype as their first product.
What complications should they anticipate, and what strategies might mitigate these?
Outline the five layers of immune defense against adenovirus discussed in Segment 4, and indicate which generation of vector engineering primarily addresses each one (or notes that none does).
